label: Seed (UI dev mock — replaced by real bank)

questions.question: Which best describes the difference between `signal()` and `computed()` in Angular?

options.text: signal() is writable; computed() is derived, lazy, and memoized.
computed() recomputes on every change detection tick regardless of dependencies.
signal() requires NgZone to track changes.
They are aliases for the same primitive.

explanations.a: Correct. signal() is the writable source of truth; computed() derives from signals, memoizes, and only recomputes when a read dependency changes AND the computed is itself read.
explanations.b: Wrong. computed() is lazy and memoized. It only recomputes when a tracked dependency changes AND the computed is read.
explanations.c: Wrong. Signals are zone-independent — they're the foundation for Angular's zoneless mode.
explanations.d: Wrong. signal() and computed() serve different purposes: source vs. derived.

questions.question: You handle a search input. Each keystroke fires an HTTP request. Which RxJS operator best prevents stale responses from overwriting newer ones?

options.text: mergeMap
switchMap
concatMap
exhaustMap

explanations.a: Wrong. mergeMap runs all inner observables in parallel; an old response can land after a new one.
explanations.b: Correct. switchMap cancels the previous inner observable when a new value arrives — exactly the right behavior for search-as-you-type.
explanations.c: Wrong. concatMap queues — keystrokes pile up and you'd see stale results in order.
explanations.d: Wrong. exhaustMap ignores new values while one is in flight — you'd drop the most recent search.

questions.question: In a zoneless Angular app, which of these will NOT trigger change detection?

options.text: A signal read in the template being updated via `set()`
A `(click)` event binding firing
A `setTimeout()` callback mutating a class property used in the template
An `async` pipe receiving a new value

explanations.a: Triggers CD. Signals are the primary CD trigger in zoneless mode — view granularity.
explanations.b: Triggers CD. Event bindings still schedule CD in zoneless mode.
explanations.c: Correct answer (does NOT trigger). Without Zone.js patching, setTimeout no longer notifies Angular. Mutating a plain property won't be picked up — you'd need a signal or manual `markForCheck`.
explanations.d: Triggers CD. The async pipe calls `markForCheck()` internally on each emission.

questions.question: What's the main benefit of `satisfies` over a type annotation?

options.text: It's a runtime check at production.
It validates against a type while preserving the narrowest inferred type of the value.
It enforces immutability of the value.
It removes the need for generics.

explanations.a: Wrong. `satisfies` is compile-time only, no runtime effect.
explanations.b: Correct. `satisfies` checks the value conforms to a type, but the variable keeps its specific inferred shape — so downstream property access is type-safe to the literal level.
explanations.c: Wrong. `as const` is what makes values deeply readonly.
explanations.d: Wrong. Generics and `satisfies` solve different problems.

questions.question: You're refactoring a large Angular component with AI assistance. Which workflow gives the BEST result for production code?

options.text: Accept all AI suggestions inline and run tests at the end.
Ask the AI to refactor, review the diff, run unit tests + type check, and gate commits on green CI.
Let the AI auto-commit each suggestion to keep velocity high.
Disable type checking during the refactor for speed; re-enable at the end.

explanations.a: Wrong. Accepting inline without diff review is how subtle regressions land. AI output requires the same review discipline as any contributor.
explanations.b: Correct. The mature pattern: AI proposes, you review the diff, the type system + tests + CI gate the change.
explanations.c: Wrong. Auto-commit removes the human review step that catches AI hallucinations and incorrect refactors.
explanations.d: Wrong. Disabling the type system removes the strongest signal that an AI-driven refactor is incorrect.

options.text: Missing trackBy on the *ngFor.
Nested subscribes leak both subscriptions and create a race condition between param changes.
It should use async/await instead of subscriptions.
The component should be marked OnPush.

explanations.a: Not the worst issue — perf concern, not correctness. The leak + race condition is more serious.
explanations.b: Correct. Both subscriptions are never unsubscribed (leak). And id=1 response may arrive AFTER id=2 — overwriting correct data. Use switchMap + takeUntilDestroyed, or toSignal().
explanations.c: Wrong. async/await doesn't address either issue. RxJS operators handle this idiomatically.
explanations.d: Useful improvement but unrelated to the bugs in the snippet.
